findings.title: Session map lock is held across `child.kill().await`
findings.description: The new teardown path removes the session with `sessions.lock().await.remove(thread_id)` directly inside the `if let` scrutinee, then awaits `inner.child.kill()`. In Rust, that temporary `MutexGuard` lives for the whole `if let` statement, so the global `sessions` mutex stays locked while the process is being killed and reaped. That blocks unrelated `get_or_create`, `send_message`, `kill_session`, and idle-sweeper calls behind an `.await`, which is an async-safety bug and can stall recovery after a hard timeout.
findings.suggestion: Drop the mutex guard before awaiting: `let inner = { sessions.lock().await.remove(thread_id) }; if let Some(mut inner) = inner { ... }`. Then perform process teardown outside the locked scope.

findings.title: Unconditional `kill().await` still misses the already-exited child case
findings.description: This cleanup always calls `inner.child.kill().await` after removing the session. If the subprocess already exited on its own and the worker is leaving because stdout hit EOF, `kill()` can fail before reaping the child. In that case the code only logs a warning and drops the `Child`, which defeats the stated zombie-prevention goal on normal exit paths. The correct teardown needs to distinguish 'already exited, just reap it' from 'still running, kill and wait'.
findings.suggestion: Use `try_wait()` first. If it returns `Some(status)`, the child is already reaped; if it returns `None`, then call `kill().await`; and if `try_wait()` errors, log that separately. That guarantees cleanup on both normal EOF and timeout-driven teardown paths.

summary: The hard-timeout/session-teardown change is directionally correct, but the new worker cleanup still has two important teardown bugs: it holds the global sessions mutex across an await, and it does not reliably reap already-exited children.
